definitions.definition: In a way or manner that is real, not unreal.
Actually; in fact; in reality.

parsedExamples.example: "He really is a true friend." / "Really? What makes you so sure?"

examples: "He really is a true friend." / "Really? What makes you so sure?"

definitions.definition: Very (modifying an adjective); very much (modifying a verb).

parsedExamples.example: But ma, I really, really want to go to the show!

examples: But ma, I really, really want to go to the show!

definitions.definition: Indicating surprise at, or requesting confirmation of, some new information; to express skepticism.

parsedExamples.example: A: He won the Nobel Prize yesterday.
A: You know, I saw Oliver the other day.
B: Really? What's he been up to?

examples: A: He won the Nobel Prize yesterday.
A: You know, I saw Oliver the other day.
B: Really? What's he been up to?

definitions.definition: A phatic confirmation by a listener, to signal active listening; mostly devoid of literal meaning, with the phatically contrived incredulity being merely formulaic.

parsedExamples.example: A: You know, I saw Oliver the other day.
B: Really? What's he been up to?

examples: A: You know, I saw Oliver the other day.
B: Really? What's he been up to?

definitions.definition: Indicating that what was just said was obvious and unnecessary; contrived incredulity

parsedExamples.example: A: I've just been reading Shakespeare - he's one of the best authors like, ever!

examples: A: I've just been reading Shakespeare - he's one of the best authors like, ever!

definitions.definition: Indicating affirmation, agreement.

parsedExamples.example: A: That girl talks about herself way too much.

examples: A: That girl talks about herself way too much.

definitions.definition: Indicating displeasure at another person's behaviour or statement.
Alternative form of re-ally.
